finding 1 of 5 · rentfrow & gosling, 2006
music is the first thing strangers talk about.
Cambridge researchers gave strangers one task: get to know each other. Out of everything they could have discussed — jobs, hometowns, interests — music was the single most common topic.

People already know, instinctively, that music tells you something no résumé or bio can. Sonar makes that instinct into infrastructure.
Rentfrow & Gosling (2006). "Message in a Ballad." Psychological Science, 17(3), 236–242.
tap to continue
finding 2 of 5 · rentfrow & gosling, 2006
a stranger can read you from your playlist alone.
In controlled studies, observers were given only a person's music preferences — no photo, no name, no context. They formed accurate personality impressions. Not guesses. Accurate ones.

Your music is not entertainment. It is a portrait. Sonar reads that portrait and finds the people whose portrait looks like yours.
Rentfrow & Gosling (2006). "Message in a Ballad." Psychological Science, 17(3), 236–242.
tap to continue
finding 3 of 5 · greenberg et al., cambridge 2015
how you think shapes what you hear.
Across 4,000+ participants, Cambridge psychologists found that your cognitive style — whether you feel deeply into others' emotions, or think in systems and patterns — predicts your music taste better than your personality does.

Your playlist is not just what you like. It is how your mind is built.
4,000+
participants
26
genres tested
Better
than Big Five
Greenberg et al. (2015). "Musical Preferences are Linked to Cognitive Styles." PLOS ONE, 10(7).
tap to continue
finding 4 of 5 · boer et al., 2011
shared music means shared values. not just shared taste.
Two lab experiments and a field study proved it: when people share music preferences, they bond — not because they enjoy the same songs, but because shared music signals shared values. The connection that forms is real, and it outperforms personality similarity as a predictor of attraction.

That is what 94% means. Not a score. A value match.
Boer et al. (2011). "How Shared Preferences in Music Create Bonds." Personality & Social Psychology Bulletin, 37(9).
tap to continue
finding 5 of 5 · the sonar thesis
your music is the most honest thing about you.
What you play at 2am. The song you've replayed 80 times. The artist you found before anyone else did.

You didn't choose those for an audience. You chose them because they matched something true inside you. That is not taste. That is identity. And for the first time, someone can actually find you through it.

sonic identity · #0031
Night Architect
Someone who builds emotional environments through sound. The playlist is not background — it is architecture. Every choice is intentional, even when it feels automatic.
2am
peak hour

top 4%
listener depth
what your catalogue says
🎤
you listen to process, not escape
Low valence, high energy. You choose music that matches emotional weight — not music that lifts your mood. This is the listening pattern of someone with a high internal life.
🔊
you move between worlds without friction
From PARTYNEXTDOOR to Kettama to Snow Strippers — no genre wall. That range is rare. It takes a specific kind of listener to hold all of that without contradiction.
📡
you find things before they find you
31% of your catalogue is in the bottom 5% of stream counts when you first played it. You are consistently early. That is a data point, not an opinion.

you
Your catalogue clusters in the high-energy, low-to-moderate valence quadrant — what psychologists call "intense and rebellious." This is the emotional signature of someone who processes complexity through sound, not simplicity. You seek music that matches emotional weight, not mood elevation.
what the data says about you
derived from audio feature patterns · cross-referenced with peer-reviewed research
listening context patterns
6-month trajectory
how your sonic signature is shifting
Your energy is rising and valence is dropping. You are moving toward higher-intensity, darker sound. Research links this trajectory to periods of heightened ambition and internal processing — typically precedes major life decisions or transitions.
sonar intelligence · learning
the model is watching
As more users join Sonar, the analysis gets sharper. Your patterns cross-reference against the growing catalogue — surfacing what's rare, what's shared, and what your music says that you haven't said yourself.

how we calculate this
Our matching model learns from every track you play, every match you make, and every conversation that clicks — continuously deepening its understanding of who you listen like. 94% means your sonic architectures are nearly identical.

9:41deep analysis · april 2025
🔬
deep analysis · tommy
Night Architect
a microscope into your musical soul. updates every month.
what your artists reveal about you
🎤
Drake / PARTYNEXTDOOR
your dominant frequency
People who gravitate toward Drake and PND are seeking emotional precision — the space between confidence and vulnerability. You likely carry a lot internally while projecting composure externally. You process in private. The playlist is architecture, not background noise.
🔊
Pawsa / Kettama / Kiki Valjiic
your underground frequency
These artists play Output Brooklyn and fabric London — not Spotify playlists. Seeking them out requires active curation. You find the room before it's full and the artist before they headline. At 128 BPM, music becomes physical — release through motion.
❄️
Snow Strippers / Somewhen
your emotional edge
Somewhere between sadness and euphoria, between a rave and the moment before dawn. People drawn here are often future-oriented — nostalgic for things they haven't experienced yet. They want to feel more than the moment they're in.
bpm zone · what your tempo says
6090120140160+
your zone: 118–132 BPM · late-night elevated
The exact overlap of hip-hop and tech house. Driven, physical, cinematic. People who live here are goal-oriented and physically expressive. You move when you listen — even subtly.
lyric themes · what your songs keep saying
🧬 subliminal signal
personality decode
"You are the person who knows the artist before the room does. You dress well but don't need the validation. That's not a flaw — it's a frequency."
monthly evolution
You started 2025 leaning heavily on Drake. By March you were pulling toward Pawsa and Kettama. April is your most eclectic month yet. You are moving outward.
listening metrics · april 2025
daily arc
6am12pm6pm12am4am
artist rotation · april 2025
You rotate through 23 artists daily. Most people max at 8. High rotation = high openness — psychologically predisposed to novelty over routine.

what they're listening to · now
how sonar calculates matches
47
Sonar reads your listening as a continuously-learning acoustic profile — track rarity, artist overlap, recency, repetition, session texture — and gets sharper with every match, message, and play.
⏱
Features are weighted by recency and repetition — what you played twice last night counts more than what you played once six months ago.
∑
Your profile cross-references against 2.1M tracks in the Sonar corpus — 8,214 listeners across 14 cities — to surface what's rare, shared, and meaningful.
why sonar gets smarter
📈
the corpus compounds
Every new user adds data that sharpens rarity scores for everyone. A song 2 users share out of 50,000 is a signal no other platform can surface.
🧬
your profile evolves
Sonar recalculates your matches every 3 minutes as you listen. A match that was 78% last week might be 94% today.
🔒
proprietary data advantage
Spotify and Apple see what you play. Sonar sees who plays the same thing near you — and what that means. Nobody else has that graph.
